metric_value: 25% of mid-market respondents admitted their companies have not been successful with IT outsourcing
notes: Startling headline statistic from Aberdeen Business Value of IT Outsourcing Benchmark Report (July 2006). None of Best in Class cited lack of success.

metric_name: Planning error stage — quantitative business case
metric_value: Best in Class: 26% error rate; Mid-Market: 33% error rate; Gap: 7 pp
notes: Figure 1: Stages Prone to Costly yet Avoidable Planning Errors. Data source: AberdeenGroup July 2006 survey.

metric_name: Planning error stage — contract pricing negotiations
metric_value: Best in Class: 11% error rate; Mid-Market: 27% error rate; Gap: 16 pp
notes: Figure 1. Contract pricing negotiations. Data source: AberdeenGroup July 2006 survey.

metric_value: Best in Class: 11% error rate; Mid-Market: 23% error rate; Gap: 12 pp
notes: Figure 1. Governance stage. Data source: AberdeenGroup July 2006 survey.

metric_name: Planning error stage — ongoing operational management
metric_value: Best in Class: 5% error rate; Mid-Market: 30% error rate; Gap: 25 pp
notes: Figure 1. Ongoing operational management — largest gap between Best in Class and mid-market. Data source: AberdeenGroup July 2006 survey.
Figure 2: How Companies Overcome Challenges to IT Outsourcing. Best in Class significantly more likely to dedicate management time and talent. Data source: AberdeenGroup July 2006 survey.

metric_name: Challenge response — escalation to outsourcer senior executive team
notes: Figure 2. Best in Class dramatically more likely to escalate when service levels fall short. Mid-market significantly less assertive. Data source: AberdeenGroup July 2006 survey.

metric_name: Challenge response — replaced initial vendor account management team
notes: Figure 2. Data source: AberdeenGroup July 2006 survey.

metric_name: Challenge response — established cross-function outsourcing management committee
notes: Figure 2. One area where mid-market and Best in Class perform identically. Data source: AberdeenGroup July 2006 survey.

metric_name: Challenge response — renegotiated contract and/or statement of work
notes: Figure 2. Mid-market slightly more likely to renegotiate. Data source: AberdeenGroup July 2006 survey.

metric_name: Challenge response — changed internal processes to reduce variable outsourcer charges
notes: Figure 2. Saia describes mid-market changing internal processes to reduce variable charges as 'the tail wagging the dog' — a sign of suboptimal outsourcing management. Data source: AberdeenGroup July 2006 survey.

metric_name: Internal knowledge gap as failure mode
metric_value: Mid-size financial services respondent cited internal 'knowledge gap' contributing to failed outsourcing — buyers must have knowledge to 'exactly state the business requirements' in RFPs
notes: Qualitative finding from survey respondent. Illustrates why RFP quality matters.

metric_value: Most Best in Class companies rely on independent consulting firms including specialized legal assistance before executing a contract
notes: Best in Class use outside consulting (legal, benchmarking, best-practices) more than mid-market firms.

metric_value: IT outsourcing is growing and mid-size enterprises that want to remain competitive should at least entertain the idea
notes: Saia's market assessment: ITO adoption growing; mid-market must participate to stay competitive.

metric_value: Best practice: hold one person responsible for overseeing outsourcing relationship with right knowledge of agreement and relationship management skills
notes: Single-point-of-accountability recommendation. Enables critical decisions without escalating to other executives.
